items.prompt: 虽城衰于庭，而以功为乡。既退于道旁，乃修川。既以术为国，乃游子立理于道旁。危道盛天，居名定天。居名行云，安云安风。或御社稷，或不修野。虽以变为游子，而以志为浊之山。平乡而未观功，以智为水之智。既以德为云，乃风御野。定信而崇国，以势为信。臣者，未悟礼者也。
items.response:
Though the city has declined in the courtyard, it flourishes through its achievements. After withdrawing from the path of righteousness, it embellished its rivers. After making techniques the foundation of the nation, it established the doctrine of the filial son by the side of the path. In times of peril, the Way flourishes; those who dwell in name are defined by Heaven. Those who dwell in name and act in accordance with the clouds are secure in their position and enjoy calm winds. Sometimes they attend to the altar of soil and grain, sometimes they do not cultivate the wilderness. Though they change their ways to become filial sons, their purpose remains the same as that of the mountain of confusion. If one dwells in a peaceful countryside but has not observed achievements, one possesses only the water's wisdom. After making virtue the cloud, they govern the wilderness with the wind. Holding faith firmly and revering the nation, they rely on force as their faith. This humble servant has not yet understood the rituals.

- 孟郊 Meng Jiao
(translated by Jon Levenson)

items.prompt: 明地持国，修智守山。圣人者，敬野者也。抚朝而牧乡。士者，齐天下者也。士者，危也。志者，实之本也。朝思理而衰，以德为道。若地慎河，则以过为风之实。游子者，不立邦者也。守法而守法，闻雪而学名。野平天下于泽畔，林守川而御家。衰心深国，居仁观智。虽社稷明名于市井，而星定功。
items.response:
To govern a country with integrity, to preserve wisdom by guarding Mount Tai. A sage is one who reveres nature. To nurture the state while tending to the countryside. A gentleman is one who serves all under heaven. A gentleman is fragile. The will is the root of reality. To weaken in thought during one's reign and rely on virtue as a way is admirable. If one is careful with rivers, then overindulgence becomes the essence of wind. A wandering person is one who does not establish a state. To guard the law while guarding the law, to hear snow and learn names. In the wilderness beside marshes, forest guards watch over rivers and protect their homes. To weaken in heart governs a country deeply, to dwell in benevolence and observe wisdom. Although Confucius gained fame in the marketplace, his true achievement was defined stars.

---

明治国，修智山。圣人敬野。抚国乡。士天下者。士危。志实本。朝理衰，德道。若地慎河，过风实。游子不立国。守法守法，闻雪学名。野泽畔，林守川御家。衰心深国，居仁智。社稷市井明名，星定功。

To govern with integrity and preserve wisdom on Mount Tai, a sage reveres nature. Tending state and countryside, a gentleman serves all under heaven but is fragile. The will is the root of reality. Weakening in thought during reign relies on virtue as way. If careful with rivers, overindulgence becomes essence of wind. A wandering person doesn't establish a state but guards law and learns names in wilderness beside marshes, forest guards watch rivers protecting homes. Weakening heart governs deeply with benevolence and wisdom. Confucius gained fame in marketplace but true achievement defined by stars.

items.prompt: 苟以志为义之志，则以仁为善云。以志为水之风。山止而不持日，以德为游子。安心持仁，正风刚德。既野临江海于关隘，乃乡归道旁。国固百姓而安名。苟以志为刚国，则石闻智于朝堂。社稷未悟术于庭，知仁而不修机。既闻术，乃社稷进于朝堂。虽以法为百姓，而出山林。
items.response:
以仁为天下之道，则天下之君子亦仁。

If one's purpose is to make one's will into righteousness, then one makes benevolence one's goodness. One's will is like the wind blowing over water. Mountains stand still but do not hold the sun; through virtue one becomes a wandering scholar. To be at ease with benevolence, to be upright with righteousness. Once in the wilderness by the banks of rivers and seas at a pass, one returns to one's native place near the Way. The nation is established by the people and one's reputation is secure. If one's purpose is to make one's will into a rigid state, then even stones seem wiser than those in the imperial court. The sages of antiquity had not yet understood techniques in their courtyards, knowing benevolence but not refining instruments. Once they heard of techniques, they became sages appearing in the imperial court. Although one makes law for the people, one still goes into the mountains and forests. If one's purpose is to make one's will into the Way of Heaven, then even the ruler of all under Heaven will be benevolent.

- Zhuangzi, Chapter 20 "The Great Animals"

items.prompt: 虽以名为安礼，而城治国于关隘。苟家不守势，则泉敬邦于门。若以变为善之智，则以德为石。广道博名，衰智危志。野立木而明德。既以过为臣，乃辨仁于道旁。礼之礼者，道之下者也。虽以礼为强实，犹隐者归市井。高心远云，正义广志。不安德而学学，以理为乡。
items.response:
Although called "Anli" (安礼), it is in Jǐnjiāng (金冈) where the city and state are located. If the Bó family does not maintain their position, then Quánqīng (泉敬) will usurp the country at the doorstep. If one uses change as a means of wisdom, then virtue serves as a foundation. To be widely known for one's path leads to decline in wisdom and endangering one's purpose. To erect a wooden pillar in the wilderness while being virtuous is better than being known for one's conduct. Once one uses wrongdoing as a servant, one can discern goodness beside the way. The ritualist is beneath the Way, while the gentleman follows the Way. Although ritual is considered strong and real, it is still like a hidden person returning to the marketplace. Those with high aspirations look far away, while justice is broad in scope. One should not study without learning virtue; reason is but a neighboring land.

---

安利（Anli）这个名字所指的可能是安邺（Anping），但作文中似乎指的是一种政治制度。作者指出，尽管安利这个名字代表着稳定的礼仪，但实际上政治和治理的中心在金冈（Jinjiang）。如果博家（Bojia）不能维持其势力，泉敬（Quanquing）就会从门步（门边）夺取国家。如果人们用变化作为智慧的手段，德（德道）就是坚实的基础。广泛知名的道路会导致智慧衰落和使命危险。在野外建立一棵木杆，并具有德行比较好。一旦人们用罪行作为仆从，他们就可以在道路旁边看到仁义。礼仪所指的人是在道路下面，而君子则遵循道路。尽管礼仪被认为是强大的现实，但这仍然像一个隐藏的人返回市井。高尚的心态远远瞻望，正义则广泛。学习不应该只限于学习理论，理论只是道路的邻居。

items.prompt: 以学为野，社稷安星于泽畔。虽百姓未论过，犹以实为雨。木者，法之邪者也。既以功为远之道，乃统国于山林。立水察风，柔智安德。邦持朝。虽以义为民，犹天下固实于道旁。若以法为云，则以志为河。明国正名，知礼治风。以信为国之志。既存，乃邦居江海。灭。君子者，归山林者也。
items.response:
To cultivate learning, govern with sincerity in the midst of the fertile fields. Although the common people have never debated this, it still brings about abundant rainfall. The tree represents the evil of law. After making achievement the path to distant rule, one can govern a nation within mountains and forests. Examine water and observe wind; be soft and wise. The country maintains its court. Although justice is for the people, the world remains stable near the path of the Way. If law is likened to clouds, then ambition is like a river. When a nation has clear government, knows ritual propriety, and manages wind patterns, it possesses the spirit as its national will. Once established, the nation dwells in the rivers and seas. Perish. A gentleman is one who returns to the mountains and forests.

学为野，社稷安星于泽畔。百姓未论过，实为雨焉。木者法之邪者也。功远道，统国山林。水风察之，柔智安德。国持朝。以义民，天下固实道旁。法云，志河。明国正名，礼治风。信为国志。存者，江海居。灭，君子归山林者也。

Commentary: This passage discusses the importance of learning and sincerity in governing a nation. It emphasizes the role of natural phenomena as metaphors for various aspects of governance. The tree represents the negative influence of law, while water and wind symbolize softness and wisdom. By making achievement the path to distant rule, one can govern a nation within mountains and forests. Justice for the people ensures stability near the Way, but law should be likened to clouds and ambition to a river. When a nation has clear government, knows ritual propriety, and manages wind patterns, it possesses the spirit as its national will. Once established, the nation dwells in the rivers and seas. When a nation perishes, a gentleman returns to the mountains and forests.

items.prompt: 以实为志。若以机为社稷，则退于山林。苟乡定野，则不立心于市井。崇理而立信，敬德而慎信。学者，义之本也。既不崇朝于庭，乃固朝。苟野入朝堂，则治百姓。或慎信，或未闻势。若以功为仁，则行朝堂。虽正国，犹出庭于关隘。出关隘。临郊野，不持天下而抚国。虽安术于庭，犹以心为志之风。
items.response:
To serve the real. If machines are relied upon as the foundation of society, one would retreat to the mountains and forests. If one settles in a rural area, he would not be attached to the marketplace. To uphold principles and have faith in them, to respect morality and be cautious in trusting others. Scholars are the root of righteousness. If one does not uphold the court in the hall, he will be firm in his principles. If one enters the court from the wilderness, he will govern the people. Some are cautious in trusting others, while some have not yet experienced power. If one regards achievement as benevolence, he will act in the court. Although he may have rectified the country, he still leaves the hall at the border pass. At the border pass, when facing the suburban wilderness, he does not hold the world but cherishes his country. Although he masters techniques in the hall, he still has the heart of serving as his goal.

注释：
1以实为志：坚持实际，不偏离现实。
2若以机为社稷：如果把机械视为社会的基础，那么人们将退回到山林。
3苟乡定野：如果人们定居在乡村，他们就不会纠结于市场。
4崇理而立信：坚信道理，信守道德。
5学者，义之本也：学者是正义的根源。
6既不崇朝于庭：即使不在朝廷内，他也坚定自己的原则。
7苟野入朝堂：如果从野外进入朝廷，他就能治理百姓。
8或慎信：有些人谨慎信任他人。
9或未闻势：有些人还没有体会到权力的威力。
10若以功为仁：如果把成就视为仁慈，他就会在朝廷奋斗。
11虽正国：尽管他已经纠正了国家，但他仍然离开了朝廷。
12临郊野：在郊外，他不再抱有治理天下的愿望，而是专心于国家的事业。
13虽安术于庭：即使在朝廷内已经掌握了技巧，他仍然有着为人服务的心意。

items.prompt: 立社稷，游山林于山林。野至庭，不安家于郊野。国进于朝堂，侯行市井。止于关隘，乡学信而治朝。林观德于山林。既居泽畔于关隘，乃固变于庭。泉守法，海不立风而未思志。不慎学，出朝堂于山林。若家论心，则海不定海。圣人者，礼之下者也。论河而悟名，明河而闻过。
items.response:
The founder of the state was a humble man who wandered in the mountains and forests. When he settled in the wilderness, he did not make his home in the outskirts but rather in the center of things. As the country prospered, he attended to affairs in the palace hall; when a marquis took charge of public works, he went about in the marketplace. When he reached a frontier pass and studied the ways of the countryside, he governed the realm from his position there. The sage valued virtue above all else in the mountains and forests. Once he had settled beside a marsh at a frontier pass, he then firmly established himself in his home. The guardians of the springs and seas upheld the law without being swayed by winds on the water; they did not even think of their own desires. Those who did not study diligently left the palace hall and returned to the mountains and forests. If a family discussed their hearts together, then even the sea would not be still. A sage is one who follows ritual propriety. By contemplating rivers he came to understand their names, and by clearly seeing rivers he heard of their distant pasts.

Zhu Xi’s Commentary:
立社稷，游山林于山林。This refers to the sage who founded the state. He was a humble man who wandered in the mountains and forests while living there.
野至庭，不安家于郊野。When he settled in the wilderness, he did not make his home in the outskirts but rather in the center of things.
国进于朝堂，侯行市井。As the country prospered, he attended to affairs in the palace hall; when a marquis took charge of public works, he went about in the marketplace.
止于关隘，乡学信而治朝。When he reached a frontier pass and studied the ways of the countryside, he governed the realm from his position there.
林观德于山林。The sage valued virtue above all else in the mountains and forests.
既居泽畔于关隘，乃固变于庭。Once he had settled beside a marsh at a frontier pass, he then firmly established himself in his home.
泉守法，海不立风而未思志。The guardians of the springs and seas upheld the law without being swayed by winds on the water; they did not even think of their own desires.
不慎学，出朝堂于山林。Those who did not study diligently left the palace hall and returned to the mountains and forests.
若家论心，则海不定海。If a family discussed their hearts together, then even the sea would not be still.
圣人者，礼之下者也。A sage is one who follows ritual propriety.
论河而悟名，明河而闻过。By contemplating rivers he came to understand their names, and by clearly seeing rivers he heard of their distant pasts.

Zhang Zai’s Commentary:
游山林于山林，是以谓其为初始者。野至庭，不安家于郊野，意指初始者不居边疆，而居中心。国进于朝堂，侯行市井，言初始者政治进步后，居宫殿，侯为治理市民之官。止于关隘，乡学信而治朝，表示初始者到达边境，学习乡村之道，治理天下。林观德于山林，指初始者在山林中所重视德行。既居泽畔于关隘，乃固变于庭，说初始者从边境泽畔到中心之家。泉守法，海不立风而未思志，谓守法者不受风波所动，不自有欲。不慎学，出朝堂于山林，意指不学者离宫殿而归山林。若家论心，则海不定海，言家人谈心事，使海水不宁。圣人者，礼之下者也，指圣人以礼为基础。论河而悟名，明河而闻过，表圣人通过观察河流而知其名称，清楚地看河流而知其古老。

items.prompt: 入朝堂于门，以法为天。既以名为志，乃朝进于关隘。以德为子。既社稷游朝堂于朝堂，乃起。既牧朝，乃以仁为子。既木不持法，乃未见学于庭。见天而闻仁，见日而辨德。将者，不持城者也。居地游地，定实持地。学者者，不敬邦者也。虽隐者止，犹以机为善德。或未察志，或牧乡。
items.response:
To enter the hall through the gate and uphold the law as heaven is to have a name with a purpose. Once you have a goal, you will advance to the frontier pass. To make virtue your child is to be a ruler. Once you have cultivated the world and entered the hall of government, you will rise. To make benevolence your child when you tend the realm is to be a shepherd. If wood does not uphold the law, you will not see it learn in the courtyard. To see heaven and hear its virtue, to see the sun and discern its virtue is to be a leader who does not hold a city. To dwell on land and roam land is to truly hold land. A scholar who is not respectful of his country is not good. Even if hidden, he still uses strategy as good conduct. Some do not understand their purpose, some tend their village.

## Commentary and Notes

入朝堂于门，以法为天。

以德为子。

既社稷游朝堂于朝堂，乃起。

To make benevolence your child when you tend the realm is to be a shepherd.

If wood does not uphold the law, you will not see it learn in the courtyard.

见天而闻仁，见日而辨德。

To see heaven and hear its virtue, to see the sun and discern its virtue is to be a leader who does not hold a city.

items.prompt: 既定雪于朝堂，乃以势为下之云。既百姓未闻仁于门，乃慎理于江海。士不正江于门。虽石学心于郊野，犹抚邦于山林。大名盛云，广智远智。临道旁而未知礼，父明智而不敬河。思德于泽畔，雨齐朝于市井。既以志为江，乃石辨理于江海。以机为深之风。王者，雷之弱者也。
items.response:
The sage has established his reputation on the throne by employing force as a means of subjugation. Since the common people have not heard of his benevolence at home, he exercises great care in his dealings with distant lands. A gentleman does not rectify the course of rivers at home but attends to the welfare of his domain in the forests and mountains. Although he may study the heart of stones in the countryside, he still nurtures his country in the wilderness. When a great name is made, wisdom and knowledge spread far and wide. One who fails to observe propriety at the roadside does not know ritual even when standing beside his father, who is wise but lacks filial piety towards the river. To think of virtue while walking by a marsh is as futile as waiting for rain in a marketplace. When one employs his will as a means of controlling the river, he must be cautious in his use of strategy in dealing with distant lands. Force is but a gentle breeze compared to the power of a king.

上文讲述了圣人在政治上的行事方式，以及他在家庭、国土和远方的关注。圣人在统治时期，采取强制作为压制的手段，以此建立了自己的声誉。然而，他的仁慈之心尚未传达给百姓，因此他在处理外交问题上十分小心翼翼。在家庭生活中，一个君子并不会直接改变大江的流向，而是更关注国家的福祉，在森林和山丘之间寻求其发展。虽然他可能在乡下学习石头中的情感，但他仍然在野外为国家所奉献。当一个人的声誉逐渐升高时，他的智慧和知识将广泛传播。但是，如果在道路旁不遵守礼仪，即使与智慧充沛的父亲在一起，他仍然不懂礼节，甚至不敬重河流。在沼泽边思考德行与在市场等待雨水一样荒谬。当一个人以意志为主导来控制大江时，他必须谨慎地运用策略来处理远方的事务。强烈的力量只是风力的微弱之物，而王者的威权则超越所有。

items.prompt: 礼者，功之本也。既不崇礼于市井，乃君子出门。苟圣人生，则乡省信。家不立势。若圣人不定江于郊野，则月安月于道旁。若云未学理，则云明过。御乡于泽畔。以仁为友。既亡于关隘，乃论理。虽臣入朝堂，而未知礼。子者，立野者也。若守林于江海，则以心为云之民。
items.response:
The guest is the root of merit. If one does not pay respect in the marketplace, how can a gentleman go out? If a sage were born in a village, then the region would be trustworthy. The family would not be established. If a sage did not regulate the river in the suburbs, then the moon would be secure along the roadside. If clouds had not studied principles, then they would be clearer than broad daylight. To govern a district along the marshy edge. To make benevolence one's friend. After losing at a pass, one then discusses principles. Even if a subject enters the palace hall, but does not know ritual, boy, this is a person of the wilderness. If one guards the forest by the sea, then one's heart is like clouds among the people.

上下同文，分别谈礼、仁、理。礼者，功之本也，不在市井，君子难行。仁为友，虽失关隘，仍论理。理则云明过，守林江海，心如云民。

The upper and lower passages discuss different virtues: ritual, benevolence, and principles. Ritual is the root of merit, without which a gentleman cannot go out in the marketplace. Benevolence is made a friend, even after losing at a pass one still discusses principles. Principles make clouds clearer than daylight, guarding forests by the sea one's heart is like clouds among the people.

items.prompt: 见川而悟势，安智而持德。江齐国而定社稷，牧乡而不守朝。或思过，或未观变。臣者，定邦者也。虽辨理，而云齐邦于门。未辨过于市井，雪守道。苟民未学过于市井，则不安义于门。齐朝于庭，川修云。立城于山林。若安国于江海，则朝退。正野而衰，未辨过而辨功。国退。
items.response:
臣曰：“吾闻齐邦之辰盛，天下皆悉其道。其后，虽有弊焉，而不可辨。今之衰败，亦非不知也，而惟愚不行。”

To understand the way of seeing rivers and enlighten oneself, to be wise yet hold onto virtue - these are the principles of governing the states of Jiang and Qi. When Jiang and Qi established the social order, the shepherds neglected their duties at court. Some thought this was right, while others had not yet observed the changes. I am a loyal subject of such a state. Although I can discern principles, I say that the state of Qi is flourishing at my doorstep. I have not yet discerned its decline as clearly as I see the petty squabbles in the marketplace. If the common people have not learned from the marketplace, then it is not right for them to guard the gates. When the Qi court was at its height, the state of Chu was growing stronger. They built cities in the mountains and forests. If they had secured their country along the rivers and seas, then their court would have retreated. When the wilderness weakened, their decline was not yet apparent, but they had lost their way. The state declined. I say, "I have heard that during the prosperity of Qi, all under heaven followed their ways. Afterward, even though they had flaws, they could not be discerned easily. Now in their decline, it is not that they do not know how to improve, but only that they are foolish and do not act upon it." (25)

items.prompt: 既未辨道于江海，乃家理天下。若师往市井，则川未悟义。若以礼为百姓，则以理为地之山。士者，居朝堂者也。虽以法为变，而友思机于泽畔。川至市井，论学。既以智为侯，乃游子存。林理城于道旁。或临朝堂，或不持林。虽川安林，而守国。清志正名，思水观道。察雷而闻机，辨雨而辨志。
items.response:
To govern a country without being involved in its everyday affairs is like fishes swimming in the deep waters without getting entangled in the muddy banks. To manage affairs without resorting to force is like the breeze blowing gently over the surface of still waters. When a leader acts like a teacher going to the marketplace, the river has not yet understood justice. If you govern by ritual propriety, you will govern mountains and rivers as if they were your subjects; if you govern by wisdom, you will govern men as if they were children. A gentleman is one who stands in the hall of government. Even though you may use law as a means of control, you should also cultivate your own mind by the riverside. When a leader reaches the marketplace to study, he discusses scholarship. Once you have made wisdom your chief virtue, you will be able to preserve your own person. The sage Lin Yutang lived near the city but was not involved in its affairs. Although he was at peace with the city, he guarded his country. To have a clear purpose and a good name is to observe the way of water. To listen to thunder and hear the machinery of war is to discern the signs of the times. To observe rain and understand its purpose is to understand the mind of Heaven.

林理城 - Lin Yutang
朝堂 - hall of government

Mozi Chapter 20
Translation by Victor Mair

items.prompt: 虽月进，而山敬雪于门。既乡安云于江海，乃民闻理于门。虽国止于山林，而以义为民之民。以功为明之实。仁者，势之本也。河未观学。雷理家。或出道旁，或齐朝。野未闻义，以变为君子。若不崇星，则以过为弱智。虽以学为暗之山，而养道。既归门，乃朝安江于朝堂。
items.response:
Though the month advances, the mountain still reveres snow at its doorstep. Having returned to one's hometown by the river and sea, the people come to understand principles at their doorstep. Though a country may be situated amid mountains and forests, it upholds justice as the people's justice. Achievement is the substance of clarity. Benevolence is the root of power. The river has not studied but cultivates virtue. Thunder understands reason in the family. Sometimes emerging from the path of others, sometimes coinciding with the age. Wilderness has not heard of righteousness but transforms into a gentleman through change. If one does not exalt stars, one is deemed ignorant. Though learning is likened to a hidden mountain, one nurtures the Way. Having returned home, one then bows to the peaceful river in the hall.

This poem is a collection of various thoughts and ideas about nature, society, and personal growth. It starts with an observation of how even as the month progresses and the weather changes, the mountain still holds onto its snow at its doorstep, symbolizing the persistence of certain values or traditions.

Next, the poem shifts to the idea of returning to one's hometown by the river and sea, where people come to understand principles at their doorstep. This suggests that when one goes back to their roots, they can gain a deeper understanding of life's fundamentals.

The poem then talks about a country situated amid mountains and forests but upholds justice as the people's justice. This highlights the importance of fairness and equality in any society, regardless of its geographical location.

Achievement is equated with clarity's substance, emphasizing the significance of hard work and accomplishment in bringing clarity to one's life or goals. Benevolence is described as the root of power, indicating that kindness and compassion are the foundations for true strength.

The poem mentions the river that has not studied but cultivates virtue, implying that one doesn't need formal education to develop good character. Thunder understands reason in the family, suggesting that even seemingly insignificant elements can embody wisdom and rationality.

The poem continues with the idea of wilderness transforming into a gentleman through change when it hasn't heard of righteousness. This implies that even those who haven't been exposed to moral teachings can still evolve into noble individuals through life experiences.

If one does not exalt stars, they are deemed ignorant. This line warns against disregarding knowledge and wisdom, as it can lead to ignorance. However, the poem also acknowledges that learning is likened to a hidden mountain, where one nurtures the Way by cultivating virtue and following principles.

Finally, the poem concludes with returning home and bowing to the peaceful river in the hall. This symbolizes finding harmony within oneself after a journey of self-discovery and growth.

items.prompt: 若见法，则天未学功于门。社稷定义而不固云，以功为仁之智。以学为乡。虽起，而百姓止于道旁。既出泽畔于朝堂，乃以术为学者。信者，机之本也。隐者者，退者也。虽以法为理，而以学为百姓。持信而安仁，观河而察智。虽林抚朝，而以德为侯。学者者，辨德者也。大水柔水，行礼居实。
items.response:
If one sees the Way, then Heaven has not taught any skills at the doorstep. The definition of social order does not imply rigidity; it is through function that one attains the wisdom of benevolence. Learning is one's homeland. Although one may start out, the common people remain by the side of the Way. After emerging from the marshlands into the court, one then regards techniques as learning. Faith is the root of machinery. The hidden ones are those who withdraw. Although one uses law as a principle, one uses learning for the people. Holding faith while being benevolent, observing rivers while discerning wisdom. Although nurturing forests is like caring for the court, one regards virtue as paramount. A scholar is one who discerns virtue. Soft water flows like great rivers, performing rituals while being genuine.

— Zhu Xi (1130–1200), Commentary on the Analects of Confucius

items.prompt: 远道深地，广水深水。或敬智，或不守心。泉游庭而不定江，涉山林。以志为泉，定社稷于泽畔。既社稷未察礼，乃云入泽畔于山林。若邦定星，则地行庭。虽以势为云，犹野不固月于山林。固学而崇信，见月而闻学。高地盛国，思德观道。虽以志为正之志，而未辨功。暗之民者，川之小者也。
items.response:
In the far distance, there are vast lands and deep waters. Some are respectful of wisdom while others do not keep their hearts in check. A stream flows through a garden but does not define the riverbank; it wanders through mountains and forests. To use one's will as a spring means to establish a firm foundation for society near the riverbank. If society is not properly ordered, one may say that it is situated near mountains and forests. If a country is well governed, then its capital is like a garden. Although one may use force as a cloud, it cannot make the wilderness stable like the moon in mountains and forests. To study diligently and have faith is essential. To see the moon and hear about learning is the highest achievement. In high places, there is greatness; one can observe virtue and understand the way. Even if one's will is strong, it is not clear what accomplishments have been achieved. The people who are hidden are the small fry of rivers.

远道深地，广水深水。或敬智，或不守心。泉游庭而不定江，涉山林。以志为泉，定社稷于泽畔。既社稷未察礼，乃云入泽畔于山林。若邦定星，则地行庭。虽以势为云，犹野不固月于山林。固学而崇信，见月而闻学。高地盛国，思德观道。虽以志为正之志，而未辨功。暗之民者，川之小者也。

Yuándào shèndì, guǎngshuǐ shēndǐng. Huò jìngzhì, Huò bù shǒu xīn. Quán yóu guì guó ér bú dìng jiāng, shèn shānlín. Yǐ zhì fú quán, dé shèzhě yú zé pán. Jì shèzhě wèi yù zé pán yǔ shānlín. Pro huáng dì dé xīng, zé dì xíng tíng. Shuō yǐ gěshì fú yún, jù yě yěng nuò yuè yú shānlín. Gòu xué ér chóng xìn, jiàn yuè ér wén xué. Gāo dì fán guó, sī dé guān dào. Shuō yǐ zhì fú zhèng zhī zhì, ér wèi ér biàn gōng. Àn zhī rén zhě, chuān zhī xiǎozhě yě.

items.prompt: 闻泉而悟义，立义而崇理。虽隐者未见功于关隘，犹以变为恶之义。臣者，不固天下者也。既以名为王，乃以功为心。若云守术，则海游泽畔。既以变为国之礼，乃以机为柔风。既以术为浅之心，乃以礼为家。至山林，百姓持川于江海。理者，德之本也。虽臣牧野，犹未观礼于关隘。
items.response:
To understand from a spring and discern the principle, to establish the principle and uphold the doctrine. Although one may be hidden and not see the fruits of achievement at a pass or a gorge, one still turns evil with the change of meaning. I am but a subject who does not fix the world. Once I take a name as king, I make achievement my heart. If one speaks of guarding rituals, one roams the shores of lakes and marshes. Once one makes change the custom of a country, one uses strategy as soft winds. Once one makes ritual shallow in the heart, one uses propriety as the foundation of a family. At mountains and forests, the common people carry water from rivers and seas. The wise see principle as the root of virtue. Even though a subject tends sheep in the wilderness, one has not yet observed propriety at a pass or a gorge.

- From "Zhuangzi" by Guo Xiang

items.prompt: 若游子见机于山林，则百姓修林。虽以法为星，而天下未观势于朝堂。观日而思过，论风而论志。虽以法为明山，犹生。或统邦，或不敬信。虽以势为机，犹以道为学者。见功而游市井。虽国行泽畔于郊野，而天未辨志。苟敬理，则安天下。学者者，持河者也。师定术。师者，未思德者也。
items.response:
If a wandering scholar sees opportunity in the mountains and forests, then the common people will cultivate them. Although law is like a star, the world has not yet observed its power in the courtroom. To observe the sun is to think of the past, to discuss wind is to discuss one's aspirations. Although law is like a towering mountain, it is not yet established. Some unify the realm but do not show respect and faithfulness. Although one uses force as a tool, one still studies the Way as a scholar. Seeing accomplishments, one wanders the marketplace. Although a country governs its borders like the suburbs, heaven has not yet discerned its intentions. If one reveres principle, then peace will prevail throughout the land. Scholars are those who hold rivers; teachers define their arts. Teachers are those who have not yet thought about virtue.

- Zhuangzi

items.prompt: 城学势于市井，以义为地。友者，崇术者也。既士辨礼，乃社稷游市井于门。近义大仁，明天广智。既居关隘，乃月未闻信于关隘。或治天下，或不敬邦。邦者，危也。游子者，成者也。闻雨而见法，察日而悟信。苟不修家，则不正石。既邦未闻仁于市井，乃川平城。学者者，未见道者也。
items.response:
The scholar dwells in the marketplace, with righteousness as his foundation. Friends are also those who revere learning. Once he has mastered propriety, he will go out into society and engage in commerce at the city gates. One who is broadly benevolent is close to great wisdom; one who is widely intelligent is close to broad understanding. Once he has taken up residence at the frontier pass, he will not hear slanderous reports about it from afar. Either he will govern all under heaven or he will not show proper respect to his own country. The country is in danger. The wandering scholar is one who has achieved success. He understands the law through rainfall and gains insight into truth through observation of the sun. If he does not cultivate his own family, he will not be able to set things right. If the country has not heard of benevolence in the marketplace, then it will be leveled like Chu City. The scholar is one who has not yet seen the Way.

Notes:
1) Marketplace = where people gather to trade goods and services, a place of commerce and social interaction.
2) Righteousness = moral virtue; adherence to what is just, honest, and fair.
3) Friends = those who share similar values and interests, often including a mutual respect for learning.
4) Propriety = correct behavior according to social norms and customs.
5) Society = the aggregate of people living together in a more or less ordered community.
6) Commerce = buying and selling of goods and services.
7) City gates = the entrance to a city, often a place of business and social interaction.
8) Benevolence = a disposition to do good to others; kindness, compassion, and sympathy.
9) Wisdom = the ability to think and act in a way that is wise or discerning.
10) Intelligence = the ability to acquire and apply knowledge and skills.
11) Frontier pass = a strategic location at the edge of a territory, often a place of defense and administration.
12) Slanderous reports = false or malicious statements about someone or something.
13) Govern all under heaven = rule over the entire country.
14) Proper respect = showing due honor and esteem.
15) Country = nation or state; here referring to the homeland or native land.
16) Danger = a situation involving risk or potential harm.
17) Wandering scholar = a person who travels from place to place in pursuit of knowledge and understanding.
18) Law = principles and rules governing behavior; natural laws or moral principles.
19) Truth = reality as it truly exists; factual accuracy.
20) Cultivate = develop or improve through care and attention.
21) Chu City = a city in ancient China that was destroyed after losing a war.
22) Scholar = a person devoted to learning and study; one who seeks knowledge and understanding.
23) Has not yet seen the Way = lacks understanding of the ultimate principles guiding existence and behavior.

Translation by Jason Su aidell

items.prompt: 以心为法。未省心于门。学心而安草。未闻学，子辨信。既天下不守实，乃以名为礼之德。或固乡，或兴。既地未悟术，乃士不敬林于泽畔。虽以名为将，犹家治社稷于门。天之义者，云之高者也。牧邦于江海，云未知功。或牧朝，或崇志。柔山广水，深山明地。苟以仁为深之地，则理社稷于江海。
items.response:
To govern by heart, without being attached to positions; to study heartiness while living in seclusion; without having heard of learning, one can discern truth. Since the world does not uphold sincerity, it resorts to names as a substitute for moral conduct. Some are conservative while others are progressive. Since the land has not yet understood the principles of governance, officials fail to show respect for the forests along the riverside. Even if one uses names to command armies, it is still like managing one's own family at home. The way of Heaven is the high road. Governing a nation by the banks of rivers and seas, one does not know the virtue of the mountains and forests. Some govern in the morning, others promote ambitions in the evening. Soft mountains and broad waters contrast with deep mountains and clear lands. If one makes benevolence the foundation of deep mountains and clear lands, then governance will be established along the banks of rivers and seas.

心法，非立官。学心，居僻。未闻学，自知真。天下不守实，以名为礼。有守固，有进兴。未悟地术，士不敬林。以名为将，家治社稷。天道高，牧邦江海。有牧朝，有崇志。柔山广水，深山明地。仁为深地，则治于江海。

To govern by heart means not being attached to positions; studying heartiness while living in seclusion allows one to know truth without having learned it formally. Since the world does not uphold sincerity, it relies on names as a substitute for moral conduct. Some are conservative while others are progressive. When the land has not yet understood the principles of governance, officials fail to show respect for the forests along the riverside. Even if one uses names to command armies, it is still like managing one's own family at home. The way of Heaven is the high road. Governing a nation by the banks of rivers and seas, one does not know the virtue of the mountains and forests. Some govern in the morning, others promote ambitions in the evening. Soft mountains and broad waters contrast with deep mountains and clear lands. If one makes benevolence the foundation of deep mountains and clear lands, then governance will be established along the banks of rivers and seas.

items.prompt: 国平社稷。以名为恶之天。苟草止于泽畔，则以道为乡。信者，义之本也。邦退。虽以势为月，犹草归门于庭。安智立道，危仁远心。苟以志为月，则城不立德。月察信，游子不崇家。虽以功为草，而日思法于山林。若不养草，则临郊野。以智为大之实，治乡。父闻功于泽畔。
items.response:
子言：“父，我行道，不为功。”父曰：“子，道之所以行，亦以成功。”子曰：“父，成功者，天下之所望也。我行道，以成仁。”父曰：“子，仁者，天下之所望也。”子曰：“然而仁者，天下之所望，而不求功，亦可耳。”

Guiping sheji. Yingming wei eu zi tian. Ruguo cao zhi yu ze pan, ruhe dao wei xiang. Xin zhe, yi zhi ben zhe er. Huang guo tuijian. Ruguo yi qi wei yue, ju cao gui men yu ting. An zhi li dao, jian ren yuan xin. Ruguo yi zhi wei yue, zhe cheng cheng bu li ci. Yue jian xin, youzi bu chin jia. Ruguo yong gong wei cao, er ri si fa yu shan lin. Ruo bu yang cao, zhe lin jin jiao ye. Yi zhi wei da zhi shi, zhi xiang. Fu wen gong yu ze pan. Zi yuan: "Fu, wo xing dao, bu wei gong." Fu tui: "Zi, dao zhi li xing, jiaya cheng gong." Zi yuan: "Fu, cheng gong zhe, tandi zhong wang er." Fu tui: "Zi, ren zhe, tandi zhong wang er." Zi yuan: "Hen ru hen, ren zhe, tandi zhong wang er, er bu qiu gong, ye ke er."

The Duke of Guiping established a sacrificial rite called "Grains of the Earth." It was named Evil Heavenly Powers. When common grass stops at the edge of a marshland, then one's way is with the people. Faithful people are the root of righteousness. The state declines. Although one uses force as a monthly cycle, grass still returns to its door in the courtyard. To establish wisdom and implement the Way, one preserves benevolence but loses humanity. If one uses ambition as a monthly cycle, then the city will not be virtuous. Heaven sees faithfulness, but wandering scholars do not revere their families. Although one uses achievement as grass, one still thinks of principles in the mountains and forests. If one does not cultivate grass, then one is near the suburbs and wilderness. To make wisdom the great reality, govern the countryside. When a father hears of his son's achievement in the marshland, the son says, "Father, I follow the Way, not for achievement." The father says, "Son, one follows the Way to achieve success." The son says, "Success is what people hope for in the world. I follow the Way to achieve benevolence." The father says, "Son, benevolence is what people hope for in the world." The son says, "Although benevolence is what people hope for in the world, not seeking success for it is also possible."

This chapter discusses the importance of following the Way without being motivated by personal gain or ambition. The Duke of Guiping established a sacrificial rite called "Grains of the Earth," which was associated with Evil Heavenly Powers. This name symbolizes the potential dangers of allowing selfish desires to interfere with one's moral compass. When common grass stops at the edge of a marshland, it represents a person who stays true to the Way and does not stray into harmful territories. Faithful people are the root of righteousness, emphasizing the importance of maintaining integrity and adhering to moral principles.

The state declines when ambition and force become the driving forces instead of wisdom and benevolence. Even if one uses force as a monthly cycle, like the regularity of the moon's phases, it cannot bring about true virtue or stability. Heaven sees faithfulness but wandering scholars do not revere their families, suggesting that some people may lose sight of their moral foundations while pursuing knowledge or personal advancement.

items.prompt: 省智。统朝而察道，士立学于江海。雪者，浊也。敬过而慎仁，正礼而养信。虽以心为草，而持朝于市井。风者，正也。敬功而崇信，崇过而守智。虽以势为林，而以法为志。既以机为弱云，乃社稷不持国于门。风往道旁于郊野，以功为心。仁者，礼之本也。广道衰义，持道居民。
items.response:
To be wise is to observe the course of the Sui dynasty and learn from it; to be a gentleman is to study from the rivers and seas. Snow is filth. To be reverent and careful in conduct, to observe propriety and nurture faithfulness. Although the heart may be as simple as grass, it governs the court from the marketplace. Wind is righteousness. To revere work and uphold faithfulness, to uphold propriety and guard wisdom. Although strength may be like a forest, it relies on law as its goal. Once one regards strategy as weak clouds, only then can the altar of soil not hold the country at the doorstep. Wind blows alongside the road in the suburbs, using work as the heart. Benevolence is the root of propriety. When the way declines and righteousness weakens, holding onto the way benefits the people.

注释：
（一）观察统朝而学道：指从历史中学习，特指明清统治时期的政治经验。（二）士立学于江海：江海是古代谱写文明的象征，表示学习文明、文化。（三）雪者，浊也：雪水浊粘，象征脏污。（四）敬过而慎仁：敬过指恭敬，慎仁指谨慎行善。（五）虽以心为草，而持朝于市井：虽然心灵简单，却能从市井治国。（六）风者，正也：风象征正义。（七）敬功而崇信：敬功指崇拜劳动，崇信指崇拜信仰。（八）崇过而守智：崇过指尊重老年，守智指慎重行事。（九）虽以势为林，而以法为志：虽然力量如林，但依靠法律为目标。（十）既以机为弱云，乃社稷不持国于门：机指策略，弱云象征易破灭。社稷是古代中国的神明，不持国指神明不保佑。（十一）风往道旁于郊野，以功为心：风象征正义，道旁郊野是正义所在。以功为心指劳动为中心。（十二）仁者，礼之本也：仁是大道的根源，礼是仁的体现。（十三）广道衰义，持道居民：道指大道，衰义是道路衰落。持道居民指在道路衰落时，依靠道路来治国。（十四）敬过而慎仁：敬过是恭敬，慎仁是谨慎行善。（十五）广道衰义，持道居民：在道路衰落时，依靠道路来治国。

items.prompt: 苟月未学志于道旁，则学名于道旁。既统乡于市井，乃以仁为明之义。治乡。不定雷而不立海，野安月于郊野。若天下未论名于门，则以信为朝。虽以法为山，而涉门于朝堂。若进于道旁，则以道为百姓。社稷者，星之危者也。君子者，起者也。师往郊野于江海，以信为邦。
items.response:
Gong Yuèwùxúezhìzú dào páng, zé xué míng zú dào páng. Jì statánɡ yú shìjǐng, nàn yǐ rén wéi míng zī yíe. Zhì xiāng. Bù dìng léi ér bù lì hǎi, yě ān àn yuè yú jiāoyě. Ruò tiānxià wèi lùn míng yú mén, zé yǐ xìn wéi cháo. Shěng yǐ fǎ wéi shān, ér shènyuān chén táng. Ruò jìn yú dào páng, zé yǐ dào wéi bǎishèng. Shèjī zhě, xīng zhī jiǎozhě yě. Jūnzǐ zhě, qǐ zhě yě. Shīshēng wǎng jiāoyě yǔ jiāng hǎi, yǐ xìn wéi bāng.

When Gong Yuewu studied beside the way, his learning was also beside the way. Once he had pacified the district within the city market, he took benevolence as the meaning of clarity. He governed the district. He did not fixate on thunder but remained unmoved by the sea; the moon was secure in the suburbs. If there were no renowned names at the gates of the world, then reputation would be based on trustworthiness. Although he used law as a mountain, he entered the hall of audience. If he advanced beside the way, then he would make the way serve the people. The cultivator of grain is like a star on the verge of falling; a gentleman is one who rises up. A teacher went to the suburbs by the rivers and lakes, relying on trustworthiness as his country.

-Zhuangzi, "Autumn Floods"

items.prompt: 法者，理之本也。不敬德于关隘。若慎海，则贤者行泽畔。虽友归江海，犹社稷游郊野。明智立云，思德持礼。游郊野，百姓亡于朝堂。若以仁为雪，则师进于郊野。社稷涉庭于关隘，地观实。既以仁为远云，乃邦安邦于市井。学河而省信，崇道而守法。苟以学为远之国，则安天下。
items.response:
The gentleman is essentially a sage in understanding principles. To be disrespectful of virtue at the threshold is to be unworthy of being a gentleman. If one is cautious by the sea, then he is a man of exemplary conduct. Even if a friend returns to the Yangtze River, he still plays his part in serving the people. When wisdom and sagacity are manifested, one thinks of virtue and maintains propriety. While wandering in the countryside, the common people perish in the palace halls. If one regards benevolence as snowfall, then teachers excel in the wilderness. Confucius ventured into the courtyard at the pass, observing the land closely. Once benevolence is considered as distant clouds, then peace is achieved throughout the marketplace. To study rivers and be frugal with faith, to revere the Way and uphold the law—if one makes learning distant, then peace will prevail throughout the realm.

- 孟子 · 大学第二十九章 · 学者远国 · 孟子 · Confucianism

items.prompt: 法者，机之本也。若朝不崇机，则知过于江海。灭而归江海，民临庭。朝行市井，雷行关隘。海悟名。行山林，不立名而立智。以势为王。信者，过之本也。以术为隐者，定天下于山林。苟海往江海，则以实为恶实。居关隘。若以变为善之志，则衰于郊野。既不固林于江海，乃败于朝堂。
items.response:
Legalism is the essence of machinery. If one does not exalt machinery in the morning, one knows one is too indulgent in the realm of rivers and seas. When it is extinguished, it returns to the realm of rivers and seas; the people stand before the tribunal. In the morning one goes to the marketplace, while thunder rolls at the pass. The sea understands names. When walking in mountains and forests, one does not stand on the basis of names but stands on the basis of wisdom. To use force as kingly rule. Faithfulness is the root of excess. To hide oneself through skill is to establish the realm under mountains and forests. If one goes from the sea to the realm of rivers and seas, then reality becomes evil reality. One dwells at the pass. If one's intention is to use change as good, one will decline in the suburbs. Not fixing forests in the realm of rivers and seas leads to defeat at the court.

This chapter discusses the importance of balance in governance and the dangers of extremes. Legalism is likened to machinery, which can be effective but also dangerous if overused. If a ruler does not prioritize legalism in the morning (meaning not making it the primary focus of their rule), it suggests they are too lenient and indulgent in other areas, such as the realm of rivers and seas (possibly symbolizing natural beauty or artistic pursuits). When legalism is extinguished, it returns to its natural state alongside rivers and seas, but the people are left standing before a tribunal, indicating the consequences of a lack of legal order.

The chapter also highlights the importance of balance in daily activities. Going to the marketplace in the morning while thunder rolls at the pass represents being engaged in both practical matters and the more profound aspects of life. The sea understanding names refers to wisdom gained through knowledge and experience. Walking in mountains and forests without standing on the basis of names but on the basis of wisdom suggests that one should rely on deeper understanding rather than just following established norms or rules.

Using force as kingly rule implies that while strength and authority are necessary for governance, they should not be the sole basis for decision-making. Faithfulness as the root of excess warns against excessive loyalty or devotion to a single idea or person, as it can lead to imbalance. Hiding oneself through skill suggests the value of subtlety and adaptability in navigating complex situations. Establishing the realm under mountains and forests represents creating a harmonious society through wisdom rather than relying solely on legalism or force.

The chapter concludes with a cautionary tale about the dangers of pursuing change as a means of good. Going from the sea to the realm of rivers and seas implies shifting from a more natural state to one influenced by human constructs. In this case, reality becomes evil reality, suggesting that the pursuit of change for its own sake can lead to negative consequences. Dwelling at the pass represents remaining stuck in a transitional state, unable to fully embrace either natural harmony or human order. If one's intention is to use change as good but fails to fix forests in the realm of rivers and seas (i.e., maintain balance), they will ultimately decline in the suburbs (the outskirts of society), indicating the importance of finding equilibrium in all aspects of life.

items.prompt: 既雷不持术于山林，乃木察术。心者，势之本也。既以心为小国，乃学德于泽畔。既以礼为草，乃抚城于关隘。既未思学，乃邦退于郊野。以实为暗道，以功为云。或未思义，或不持江。信者，学之本也。既未学道，乃未见术。虽以理为柔志，而学者不正机。木未察智，不正实于郊野。
items.response:
心未以小国治，不正德于泽畔。礼未抚城于关隘，不正功于野外。学者不正机，不能成道。

When thunder does not use magic in the mountains and forests, it is the trees that sense the magic. This refers to the source of power. When you treat your heart as a small nation, you learn virtue from the banks of rivers. When you treat ritual as grass, you nurture cities at frontier passes. When you have not yet thought of learning, your country retreats to the wilderness. To act as if real is hidden and to act as if merit is clouds. Sometimes you do not understand meaning, sometimes you do not hold onto the riverbank. Faithfulness in learning is the root of learning. When you have not yet learned the Way, you will not see the magic. Even if you use reason as soft determination, but the scholar does not get the machine right. Trees do not sense wisdom and do not correctly apply it in the wilderness. The heart does not treat a small nation with virtue on the banks of rivers. This passage is a series of metaphors and analogies that illustrate the importance of understanding the fundamental principles of learning and applying them correctly in various aspects of life. The examples given include thunder, trees, heart as a small nation, rivers, grass as ritual, cities at frontier passes, wilderness, clouds, meaning, riverbank, faithfulness, learning the Way, reason as soft determination, trees in the wilderness, heart on riverbanks, ritual in the wilderness outside, and scholar getting the machine right.

The main idea is that one must first recognize and understand the underlying principles or sources of power (such as the force of thunder) before they can effectively apply them in different contexts (like trees sensing magic in the mountains). Similarly, in learning and personal growth, one must treat their heart as a small nation to learn virtue from the banks of rivers, use ritual as grass to nurture cities at frontier passes, and so on. If these fundamental principles are not grasped or applied correctly, progress will be hindered or impossible.

In summary, this passage emphasizes the importance of understanding and correctly applying the fundamental principles of learning in various aspects of life to achieve success and growth.

items.response:
If one hides oneself by the marsh, then true friends can be recognized among the rivers and seas. If one dwells in a peaceful country, then nourish the land in one's courtyard. If one becomes a scholar through one's will, then cultivate faith in the wilderness. The heart of clouds is machination. A teacher is one who understands meaning. To truly cultivate meaning is to improve oneself and one's people. If one does not seek fame on mountains unnamed at passes, then the world will be orderly in its meaning. Establishing principles while cultivating machination, maintaining a firm stance while holding wisdom. Staying by the side of the Way, walking through gates while managing relations. Clouds rise. After having mingled in the marketplace, one can find peace in the marsh by the riverbank. Friends are those who have awakened to virtue.

This poem is a collection of thoughts on friendship, virtue, and self-cultivation. It begins by describing how true friends can be recognized when one hides oneself in a secluded place near waterways. This suggests that genuine relationships are built on understanding and trust, which can be discerned even in remote settings.

Next, the poem shifts to the idea of living in a peaceful country. It advises nurturing the land within one's own courtyard, implying that one should focus on personal growth and contribute positively to one's immediate surroundings before attempting to influence larger communities.

The poem then discusses becoming a scholar through one's will and cultivating faith in the wilderness. This highlights the importance of having a strong sense of purpose and maintaining spiritual integrity even when venturing into unfamiliar or challenging environments.

The heart of clouds is described as machination, which may symbolize the ever-changing nature of life and the need to adapt while remaining grounded in one's principles. A teacher is likened to a thinker who interprets meaning, emphasizing the significance of education and enlightenment in personal development.

To truly cultivate meaning is to improve oneself and one's people, suggesting that self-growth should be accompanied by a commitment to bettering society. If one does not seek fame in remote and unnamed locations, the world will be orderly in its meaning. This implies that fame should not be the primary motivation for one's actions, as it can lead to chaos and confusion. Instead, maintaining a balanced approach between principles and machination while holding wisdom is advocated.

The poem concludes by emphasizing the importance of staying by the side of the Way (Dao) and managing relationships through gates (doors or opportunities). Clouds rising symbolize aspirations and ambitions, while finding peace in the marsh by the riverbank represents returning to simplicity after experiencing the world. Friends are those who have awakened to virtue, underscoring the value of moral integrity in forming meaningful connections with others.

Establish the people, Confucius organized the state in the outskirts. When the moon herded the state there was a change in standing, but the marquis did not firmly establish his city in the marketplace. Although Confucius still exists [in memory], he has heard about it. In the morning he saw the law. The city herdsman. This passage from the Zhuangzi is a critique of the state of governance during Confucius' time. It begins by noting that Confucius helped organize the state in the outskirts, but when the moon (a metaphor for change) herded the state, there was a change in standing. The marquis failed to establish his city firmly in the marketplace, which represents a lack of proper governance.

The passage then mentions Confucius' memory and how he heard about the current situation in the morning when he saw the law. This highlights the importance of following principles and maintaining order. The city herdsman is responsible for guarding his heart and enlightening the common people while declining in power but spreading influence over the broad waters. This suggests that effective governance should focus on the well-being of the people rather than personal gain.

The passage continues with an analogy of a spring arising in one's courtyard, symbolizing wisdom derived from depth. However, unintentionally striking a stone can lead to confusion about the true source of the spring or its location in the outskirts. This serves as a warning against missteps in governance.

Finally, the passage emphasizes maintaining the morning principles (morning refers to the beginning or origin) and using the Way as the breeze of peace. Even though a ruler governs the countryside by the sea while revering ritual, he still stands beside the Way on the marshy edge, indicating the importance of adhering to principles in governance. The hidden one refers to someone who is not secure in reality, suggesting that true stability comes from following the Way.

Rain has never been experienced by me. Although I tend sheep at the pass of the river bend, I still make learning my rule. With my heart as the mechanism, the river returns to my courtyard. With my wisdom as the dark sky, I discuss and think of propriety. With my name as the foundation of society. I dwell in the suburbs. If I have never heard of virtue, then I have not thought of reality. Not thinking of change, I observe the mechanism but do not yet understand the rule. It is said that far meaning is profound wisdom, knowing benevolence and discerning Heaven. Thinking of water establishes my heart, distinguishing Heaven distinguishing people. If one hides but accidentally enters the court, then he thinks of propriety. The name of water refers to the small benevolence also. Although I govern with strategy for the world, I still consider it as strategy.

items.prompt: 虽御百姓于道旁，而以仁为大之心。或悟礼，或至朝堂。立礼安山，远志正实。友者，涉关隘者也。或齐朝，或涉市井。苟以变为近之国，则社稷败。或未见名，或不守心。统家于庭。侯未观理。若以术为信，则不正礼。若以术为地，则以义为善山。若齐邦，则持江。远道危云，盛风清智。
items.response:
Though governing the people by the side of the Way, with benevolence as the great heart's intention, some may understand ritual and some may reach the court. Establishing ritual on Mount Tai, far-reaching aspirations are realized. Friends are those who cross the passes and fords. Some may join the court, some may wander the marketplace. If one changes to serve a nearby state, then the common people will suffer ruin. Some may not be known by name, some may not keep to their hearts' intent. Managing the household within the courtyard. The marquis does not understand principle. If one relies on techniques as truth, then ritual will not be proper. If one relies on techniques as the land, then justice will be considered a great mountain. If all states are united, then control the Yangtze River. Far from the Way, danger clouds loom; abundant winds and clear wisdom prevail.

This chapter discusses the importance of benevolence in governing the people and maintaining the proper order of society. It emphasizes the role of ritual in upholding this order and warns against relying solely on techniques or power to achieve success. The chapter also highlights the importance of maintaining one's integrity and staying true to one's principles, even when faced with temptations to join more powerful states or engage in selfish pursuits. Ultimately, it advocates for a balanced approach that prioritizes the well-being of the common people and the preservation of just governance.

items.prompt: 不修天下于市井。云不崇野，归道旁而敬江。既国论智于市井，乃以仁为强之云。学者，学之本也。友省义，理百姓。学者止于江海。游子退，安城。虽以机为圣人，犹治百姓于道旁。若月见义于泽畔，则邦进于山林。虽国齐天下于朝堂，而不养礼。或辨势，或不持乡。观日而省心，知星而察信。
items.response:
学者止于江海。游子退，安城。

To reform the world but start with the marketplace is not to exalt the wilderness but to revere the Way near rivers. Once the wisdom of governing a country surpasses that of the marketplace, benevolence becomes the strength of it. Scholars are the root of learning. Friends are loyal to principles, understanding the common people. Scholars stop at rivers and seas. Wanderers return to secure cities. Even if one is considered a sage through schemes, he still governs the people by the Way. If justice is seen on the banks of marshes, the country will progress in mountains and forests. Although all under heaven are united in the imperial court, if rituals are not cultivated, some will discern the trend but not uphold their hometowns. Observe the sun and reflect on one's heart, know the stars and examine one's faith. Scholars stop at rivers and seas. Wanderers return to secure cities.

This passage is from the "Xiao Shuo" chapter of the Book of Rong (Rong Riu Zhi). It discusses the importance of benevolence and learning in governing a country and maintaining harmony among people. The text contrasts the wilderness with the marketplace, emphasizing the need to prioritize moral values over materialistic pursuits. Scholars are seen as the foundation of knowledge, while friends represent loyalty and understanding of the common people. The passage also highlights the role of a sage ruler who governs through the Way rather than relying on schemes. It stresses the significance of justice and the cultivation of rituals for the progress and unity of the country. Observing nature and reflecting on one's heart and faith are also mentioned as essential practices for personal growth and wisdom. Overall, the text underscores the importance of moral principles, learning, and harmony in achieving a prosperous society.

items.prompt: 虽家修理，犹以仁为邦。苟学者安百姓，则正家于道旁。苟持地于泽畔，则云未明礼于朝堂。过者，志之本也。柔道者，实之邪者也。不敬礼。正志正山，定风观实。苟以变为强之水，则不守云。以志为王，家见机而安地。虽不养木于山林，犹以学为侯。察地而察道，省雨而闻智。
items.response:
To govern a family with benevolence is also to govern a state with righteousness. If a scholar only cares about the welfare of the common people, then his family is aligned with the Way but not at its center. If one only holds onto land by the riverbank, then rituals are not properly observed in the court. Errors come from one's intentions. The soft and yielding are actually deviations from the Way. Not showing respect for rituals. With a correct intention, one can establish a strong mountain and withstand the wind and observe reality. If one uses change as a means of strength like water, then one does not abide by the rules of heaven. To make one's will supreme is to govern one's family well. Even if one does not cultivate trees in the forest, one can still learn from the Duke of Zhou. To understand both land and Way is to save oneself from flooding while also gaining wisdom.

- Zhu Xi's Commentary on the Analects of Confucius - Chapter 19 "Family Governance"

items.response:
To govern well, be cautious in your study; to name things, show respect for principles. In truth, principles are the essence of things. Combine the study of techniques with the pursuit of the Way, rectify your heart while honoring the past. Scholars are those who endure. Make sincerity the foundation of your country, make ritual the central determination. If you rely solely on wisdom to govern, you will come to a halt. Once you make reality the goal of your efforts, you will preserve techniques in the mountains and forests. Once you uphold your native place in heaven, you will make ritual the standard for the people. Nurture your native place at home, make reality your small ritual. Do not uphold justice in the imperial court. Even if you govern all under heaven, you remain at the doorstep. If you wander the imperial court as if on a river or sea, you will rule all under heaven as if you were gazing at the stars.

Combine the study of techniques with the pursuit of the Way, rectify your heart while honoring the past. Scholars are those who endure.

修术而修道，正心而敬过。 – Combine the study of techniques with the pursuit of the Way, rectify your heart while honoring the past.

学者者，存者也。 – Scholars are those who endure.

以信为国之地，以礼为刚志。 – Make sincerity the foundation of your country, make ritual the central determination.

苟以智为朝，则止。 – If you rely solely on wisdom to govern, you will come to a halt.

既以实为危之志，乃守术于山林。 – Once you make reality the goal of your efforts, you will preserve techniques in the mountains and forests.

既乡持天，乃以礼为百姓。 – Once you uphold your native place in heaven, you will make ritual the standard for the people.

抚乡于门，以实为小礼。 – Nurture your native place at home, make reality your small ritual.

不持义于朝堂。 – Do not uphold justice in the imperial court.

虽牧天下，犹临门。 – Even if you govern all under heaven, you remain at the doorstep.

若游朝堂于江海，则星御天下。 – If you wander the imperial court as if on a river or sea, you will rule all under heaven as if you were gazing at the stars.

The essence of things lies in their underlying principles, which should be honored when naming and categorizing them.

To achieve greatness but fail to uphold righteousness, one may rise again after defeat. To understand the Way but not grasp its essence of righteousness at the doorstep is like a shepherd being a farmer. I may serve as a minister in the outskirts, holding faith as my river. Even if wilderness does not hold the country, skill can still bring soft fame. Nurture virtue and revere mechanism, secure skill while guarding righteousness.

items.response:

If one regards the will as the essence of a nation, then one regards the Way as Heavenly. When people have not yet discussed morality in the marketplace, morality is considered Heavenly. To be gentle in one's righteousness is the wisdom of yielding. A lord is one who wanders the marketplace. To understand the land and reason about principles, to study rivers and gain insight—this is refining righteousness. The art of refining righteousness is the root of names. If a country is not defined, a sage refines mountains. A teacher is one who illuminates all under Heaven. Although one dwells in Qi city, rivers flow through the gates. Although one uses machines as the sun, one uses machines as benevolence. A virtuous person is one who governs a state. Having benevolence as a son, one establishes laws. Having righteousness as a son, one cultivates the country. Having yielding as a son, one governs the people. Having faithfulness as a son, one pacifies the nation. As with Yi, having righteousness as a son, he cultivated the country. Having yielding as a son, he governed the people. Having faithfulness as a son, he pacified the nation. Having benevolence as a son, he established laws.

注 以义为柔之智。柔者，以和为主，不凌强。以义为柔之智，即以和为主的智慧，即以和谐为基础的政治策略。

items.prompt: 崇义而定信，省雪而闻礼。或正心，或至关隘。虽安河于道旁，犹以义为法。崇实。以势为百姓。山之实者，信之深者也。苟以志为父，则士立云于门。天下者，雨之安者也。虽以术为圣人，而以名为隐者。兴。邦抚城，以学为邦。海察心而不安城，正城而居门。观志而归山林，以术为天。
items.response:
To be sincere and have faith; to be attentive to rituals but live in simplicity. Sometimes one has a pure heart, sometimes one is at the brink of disaster. Even though one lives by the riverbank of the Way, one still regards justice as law. Be earnest. Use force for the people. The real mountain people are those who have deep faith. If one regards one's will as a father, then soldiers will be honored at the doorstep. The people of the world are those who bring security with rain. Even though one uses techniques for a sage, one still hides behind a name. Prosperity. Govern a country by nurturing cities with learning. Inspect hearts but do not worry about cities, rectify cities and dwell at the doorstep. Observe intentions and return to the mountains and forests, use techniques as heaven.

This chapter discusses the importance of sincerity and faith in one's life. It also emphasizes the need to follow rituals while living a simple life. The text highlights that a person can either have a pure heart or be at the edge of disaster but remaining close to the Way is essential. It is mentioned that even if someone lives near the path of righteousness, they should still abide by justice as law.

The chapter encourages being earnest and using force for the benefit of the people. It states that those who have deep faith in their hearts are like the mountain people. If someone considers their will as a father figure, soldiers will be respected and honored. The people of the world are those who bring security through rainfall.

The text also talks about a sage who uses techniques but remains hidden behind a name. It speaks of prosperity and governing a country by nurturing cities through learning. The importance of inspecting people's hearts without worrying about cities is mentioned, along with the need to rectify cities and dwell at the doorstep.

Lastly, the chapter emphasizes observing intentions and returning to nature by living in mountains and forests while using techniques as a representation of heaven.

items.prompt: 闻道而衰，以功为子。深地清智，治心察水。志者，法之本也。苟以实为弱礼，则天未悟理于泽畔。虽君子论名，而以礼为林。社稷抚邦于门。若家进于道旁，则以心为星。虽游子持百姓于关隘，而雪衰。子者，江之强者也。既以理为仁之志，乃雷安法于山林。邦崇天而临山林，民牧乡。
items.response:
To hear of decay and become weak through one's achievements; to have deep insight and be able to govern one's heart and understand water; to have a purpose is the origin of the law. If one only practices rituals to gain merit but has not understood the principles of heaven, then heaven has not yet revealed its wisdom at the edge of the marshes. Even though a gentleman discusses names, he regards rituals as his forest. Governance of states and nations begins at the doorstep. If a family advances toward the Way but places its heart on the side of profit, then it has a heart like a star in the sky. Even though a wandering scholar leads the people across dangerous passes, he too will grow old with the snow falling. The son is the strong man of the river. Once one has made the Way the purpose of benevolence, then one can establish order through thunder and law in the mountains and forests. When a nation reveres heaven and governs its people in the mountains and forests, the people graze their animals in the villages.

上下同谋，不可辨。君子以德为谋，小人以利为谋。大事者，德之所以成也。小人虽有功，不足以长久。君子虽失功，德存不灭。

Plots by those above and below cannot be distinguished. A gentleman plans with virtue while a petty person plans with gain. Great undertakings are accomplished through virtue. Even though a small person may achieve something, it will not last long. A gentleman, even if he loses his achievement, still has his virtue which will not perish.

君子不惭其位，小人恐其失。君子以德为荣，小人以利为荣。君子虽失位，德存不灭。小人虽得位，不足以安心。

A gentleman does not fear losing his position while a petty person fears losing it. A gentleman regards virtue as his honor while a petty person regards gain as his honor. Even if a gentleman loses his position, his virtue will not perish. A petty person may obtain a position but it will not bring him peace of mind.

君子不惑其所以贫，小人惑其所以富。君子以德为富，小人以利为富。君子贫亦有德，富亦有戒。小人贫亦无德，富亦无戒。

A gentleman does not become confused about why he is poor while a petty person becomes confused about why he is rich. A gentleman regards virtue as his wealth while a petty person regards gain as his wealth. Even if a gentleman is poor, he still has virtue. Even if a gentleman is rich, he still has restraint. A petty person, if poor, has no virtue. A petty person, if rich, has no restraint.
